title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Colt Detective Special is a carbon steel framed double-action short-barreled revolver, and is an example of a class of firearms known to gun enthusiasts as "snubnosed", "snubbies", or "belly guns".

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: It is one of the quintessential snub-nosed revolvers and has since been discontinued, with many asking that it be brought back.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: It was seen in Beverly Hills Cop.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: As the name "Detective Special" suggests, this model revolver was used as a concealed weapon by plainclothes police detectives chambered for .38 Special.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Detective Special was initially available in both bright blued and nickel finishes; a stainless steel finish replaced the nickeled option during the Fourth Series.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: For the Second Series, caliber options were .32 New Police, .38 New Police, and .38 Special; only .38 Special was offered for the other Series.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The standard barrel length was 2 inches, but also a (rare) three-inch-barrel was offered during the Second and Third Series.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Introduced in 1927, the Detective Special was one of the first short-barreled revolvers produced with a modern swing-out frame.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: It was designed from the outset to be chambered for higher-powered cartridges.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Detective Special is by design a shortened and somewhat streamlined Colt Police Positive Special, sharing that revolver's slim 'D' size frame and six round capacity.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The DS uses a slightly smaller frame than the Colt Official Police or Smith & Wesson 'K' Frame revolvers, but is larger than the compact 'J' frame used in Smith & Wesson five-shot revolvers.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Detective Special went through several issues or series.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The First Series was produced from 1927 until 1946.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Compared to later production models, the First Series used a narrower frame, with reduced clearance between the frontstrap of the gripframe and the rear of the trigger guard.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Other distinctive features included a shorter ejector-rod with an ungrooved, knurled tip; a checkered hammer spur and cylinder latch, a "half-moon"-shaped front sight, and an overlapping screw and locking pin set-up on the right side of the frame.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Grip panels were wooden.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: A rounded butt on the metal frame became standard in 1933, but pieces with the original square butt (like that of the Police Positive Special) continued to be produced into the 1940s.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Second Series ran from 1947 to 1972.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The ejector-rod was longer and had a groove in its knurled tip; a three-inch-barrel variant was offered, with a yet longer ejector-rod.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The cylinder latch was smooth, and the trigger spur serrated.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The right side frame screw has no locking pin, and the rear half of the front sight is a serrated ramp.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The grip panels were plastic in 1947, but were changed back to wood starting in 1955 (first with a silver-tone Colt medallion, and later a gold-tone).

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: An optional hammer shroud was available from the factory to prevent the hammer from catching on clothing.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The transition from First to Second Series was gradual, with some post-WWII Second Series guns retaining short ejector rods and checkered hammers.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Because of this, assigning a given revolver to a particular issue is best done by serial number.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: During the 1960s, the grip frame of the Second Series Detective Special was shortened, matching that of Colt's other snub-nosed pistols, the Cobra and Agent.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Despite this alteration, the Detective Special's overall grip size remained unchanged, as Colt fitted the Second Series with new, lengthened gripstocks that extended below the frame.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Third Series ran from 1973 to 1986.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: A new shroud extended down from the barrel, enclosing and protecting the ejector-rod, and the front sight was changed to a full ramp.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: New, oversize wood gripstocks were introduced that covered the front frame strap.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Third Series featured improvements to the revolver's internal lockwork as well.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: As with the previous two Series, a few nickel-plated guns were produced, and a 3-inch-barrel variant was again offered.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: In 1986, facing stagnant sales numbers as well as rising production and labor costs, Colt discontinued production of the Detective Special.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Colt filed for bankruptcy protection in 1992.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: After reorganization, the company restarted production of the Detective Special in 1993.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The post-1992 Detective Special is sometimes called the Fourth Series, and featured "composite" (rubber), wrap-around grips with a gold medallion.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Only a two-inch barrel was offered, in blue or hard chrome finish.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The new production run continued only until 1995, when Colt introduced its stainless-steel SF-VI as a replacement for the Detective Special.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: From its introduction, the Detective Special used Colt's ‘Positive Safety Lock’ (hammer block), first featured on the Police Positive; the mechanism interposes a bar between hammer and frame until the trigger is pulled, preventing accidental discharge if the hammer is struck (e.g., if a dropped gun falls onto its hammer) with the trigger forward.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: First and early Second Series Detective Specials are becoming highly sought after by collectors, particularly if they are in prime condition and still have the famous Colt 'Royal Blue' finish.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: One early variant based on the DS frame was the Colt Banker's Special.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: First produced in 1928, it was chambered in .38 Colt New Police (.38 S&W) and .22 Long Rifle.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: Few were made, particularly in .22LR caliber.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Banker's Special was popular with railway clerks, who often carried them on mail and parcel freight trains prior to World War II.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: During World War II production was discontinued, and the type was not revived following the war's end.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The Colt Commando Special was a version of the Detective Special with a matte finish and rubber grips; produced from 1984 to 1986, it was chambered in .38 Special and weighed 21.5 oz.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: During the Fourth Series production run of 1993–1995, Colt offered the Detective Special with an optional de-spurred 'bobbed' hammer and double action only lockwork, direct from the factory.

title: Colt Detective Special
sentence_text: The DAO or 'Bobbed Hammer' Detective Special was otherwise the same as the standard Fourth Series Detective Special.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 10
sentence_text: The Smith & Wesson Model 10, previously known as the Smith & Wesson .38 Hand Ejector Model of 1899, the Smith & Wesson Military & Police (which would later be used in their more recent line of weapons) or the Smith & Wesson Victory Model, is a .38 Special revolver of worldwide popularity.

sentence_text: It was the successor to the Smith & Wesson .32 Hand Ejector Model of 1896 and was the first Smith & Wesson revolver to feature a cylinder release latch on the left side of the frame like the Colt M1889.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 10
sentence_text: In production since 1899, it is a six-shot double-action revolver with fixed sights.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 10
sentence_text: Over its long production run it has been available with barrel lengths of 2 in (51 mm), 3 in (76 mm), 4 in (100 mm), 5 in (130 mm), and 6 in (150 mm).

sentence_text: Barrels of 2.5 inches (64 mm) are also known to have been made for special contracts.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 10
sentence_text: Some 6,000,000 of the type have been produced over the years, making it the most popular centerfire revolver of the 20th century.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 10
sentence_text: It was later replaced by improved, more aesthetically-pleasing revolvers such as the Smith & Wesson Model 15 and the Smith & Wesson Model 19 (the Model 15 chambered for .357 Magnum), however, variants are still produced today, including the stainless steel Model 10, the Model 64.

sentence_text: It was introduced in 1978 and a Mag-Na Ported Model 629 with a snub-nose barrel was used by Victor Maitland in Beverly Hills Cop as his personal sidearm.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: The Dan Wesson Model 15 is a revolver produced by the Dan Wesson company, created by Daniel B.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: Wesson II, the great-grandson of one of the founders of Smith & Wesson.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: Dan Wesson Firearms is known for its revolver expertise and craftsmanship.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: It was seen in Beverly Hills Cop.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: The Dan Wesson Model 15 began as the W12, or the Dan Wesson Model 12 which was chambered in .357 Magnum.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: The barrels and shrouds for this model were interchangeable and used an exposed nut on the muzzle end to secure the barrel and shroud.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: The shrouds on these early models had an elongated flange which helped to center the barrel and properly secure it.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: Later, this design was refined into the Model 15 which still used the flanged barrel assemblies, but which had the nut recessed inside the flange in order to give the pistol a more conventionally finished look.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: Further refinements to the model 15 resulted in the Model 15-2 which is the most well known as well as the best selling Dan Wesson model.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: The 15-2 used a roll pin inserted into the frame as a centering dowel combined with a precisely drilled hole in the shroud assembly to facilitate proper shroud centering and alignment, thus eliminating the flanged barrel shrouds.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: The 15-2 also introduced more barrel offerings, including lengths of 2.5, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 15 inches, venting, ribbed shrouds, shrouds with full underlugs, and plain solid shrouds.

title: Dan Wesson Model 15
sentence_text: The pistols could be ordered as "Pistol Pacs" with 3 (initially) and later 4 (or more) barrels shipped inside a fiberglass briefcase with barrel changing tool and clearance gauges; however, many pistols were sold with only one barrel- though owners could choose to purchase other barrels later.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 27

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 27
sentence_text: The Model 27 was built on Smith & Wesson's carbon steel, large N-frame, and was available at various times with 31⁄2", 4", 5", 6" or 83⁄8" barrel lengths and had adjustable sights.

sentence_text: It was seen in Beverly Hills Cop II.

sentence_text: When first introduced by Smith & Wesson in 1935 it was known as the Registered Magnum.

sentence_text: The model was essentially a custom order revolver.

sentence_text: Barrel lengths could be had in quarter-inch increments from 3.5" to 8.75" in length.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 27
sentence_text: In addition to the different lengths of barrels available there were different grips, front sights, triggers, hammers, and finishes available.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 27
sentence_text: Each Registered Magnum came with a certificate of authenticity.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 27
sentence_text: Even though it was introduced in the middle of the Great Depression and was extremely expensive, Smith & Wesson found itself backlogged with orders for the four years that it produced the Registered Magnum.

title: Smith \u0026 Wesson Model 27
sentence_text: The Kansas City Police Department issued the Registered Magnum to its officers, and many other law enforcement officers across the United States carried the Registered Magnum.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: The Colt Trooper is a medium frame double-action revolver featuring a six-round cylinder, chambered for .22 and .38 caliber cartridges.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: It was designed as a less expensive alternative to the upscale Colt .357 and the later Python, it was marketed to law enforcement agencies as well as civilian firearms enthusiasts and collectors.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: A Colt Trooper Mark III was seen in Beverly Hills Cop II.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: The Colt Trooper was introduced to the firearms market by the Colt's Manufacturing Company in 1953, the Trooper and its high-end cousin, the .357 Magnum, were introduced with the intention of addressing the medium frame revolver market, as law enforcement officers had long complained about the weight of earlier models.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: The two guns were seen as ideal in size and handling characteristics for the .38 Special and its big brother the .357.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: Offered as an alternative and competitor to Smith & Wesson’s Model 28 "Highway Patrolman", the Colts were lighter and handier.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: The Mark III model, which was seen in Beverly Hills Cop II was manufactured from 1969 to 1982.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: In the late 1960s, Colt began to be concerned with a decline in its market share because of price increases brought about by the high labor costs inherent in its manufacturing processes.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: In response, an entirely new product line of revolvers dubbed the MK III series debuted in 1969.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: Intended to be the first major advancement of Colt’s designs since the beginning of the 20th Century, the MK IIIs used a new ‘J’ frame and had no parts interchangeability with older models.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: The new revolvers were considered groundbreaking as they were the first modern revolver designs to employ a state of the art transfer-bar lockwork system.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: This lockwork was not only more sophisticated, but inherently safer due to its superiority to the older hammer-blocking designs; the revolver could fire only if the trigger was deliberately pulled completely to the rear.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: It also vastly improved on the earlier design in durability, and offered the advantage of employing sintered iron internal parts rather than expensive forged ones.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: The sintered parts also allowed for improved fabrication tolerances, and could be given a special heat treatment resulting in a harder more wear-resistant composition.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: Using these parts virtually eliminated hand fitting, significantly lowering labor costs associated with the assembly and manufacture of the MK III line.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: The springs used in the Mark III internals were also an improvement.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: Unlike the older flat style, they were coiled and made entirely of corrosion-resistant stainless steel.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: The MK III series incorporated a number of models, several of which were updates of existing designs.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: Classic models included the venerable Colt Official Police chambered in .38 Special as the basic/entry-level offering, and the Trooper in .357 Magnum.

title: Colt Trooper
sentence_text: New members of the line up included the Lawman, Metropolitan Police, and Border Patrol.

title: Jericho 941R

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Despite these names being used in the US market, the Jericho 941 is not related to the IMI Desert Eagle other than its manufacture and design by IMI, and bears only a slight cosmetic resemblance to the larger pistol.

sentence_text: From January 2009 until they ceased business in January 2010, K.B.I., Inc. (which also imported Charles Daly firearms) imported of the handgun as the Jericho.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Magnum Research, now a division of Kahr Arms, announced a renewed importation of the Jericho.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The original Jericho 941 was modeled on the well-respected CZ-75 pistol designed and produced by Česká zbrojovka (CZ) of the Czech Republic and built using parts supplied by the Italian arms house Tanfoglio, which had been making their own CZ-75 clones.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Using a well-tested design allowed IMI to avoid the teething problems most new pistol designs experience, and subcontracting much of the basic fabrication work to Tanfoglio allowed IMI to quickly and economically put into production a pistol that would have enough Israeli content to satisfy government contract requirements.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: While the R-versions of Jericho 941 feature a combined safety/decocker, which on safe also decocks the pistol, the decocker version of CZ-75 (CZ-75BD) features only a simple de-cocker.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The barrel of the CZ-75 is traditionally rifled, while the Jericho 941 features a polygonal barrel, furthermore the Jericho 941 is substantially heavier.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: These differences translate into substantial differences in the condition in which the gun is carried.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Magazines for the CZ-75 and Tanfoglio T95 will function in the Jericho 941.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: One innovation by IMI was a new, much "hotter" cartridge, the .41 Action Express (see below) to go along with the Jericho 941.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The difference between 9mm and .41 AE is the same as .44 Magnum and .50 AE.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The AE bullets have rebated rims which are the same diameter as the less powerful rounds, but the casing is wider, providing more power.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: This allows these pairs of calibers to be used in the same firearm with only a change of barrel, recoil spring and magazine.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The .41 AE was less commercially successful than the 10 mm, and was soon discontinued.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Experience with heavily loaded rounds gave IMI a considerable lead, however, in chambering for the soon-to-be successful .40 S&W and also allowed the Jericho to "up-chamber" to the very popular .45 ACP.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The Jericho 941 design has been modified to include accessory rails on the frame for mounting lasers or flashlights, a feature found on many current-generation semi-automatic handguns.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The introduction of the Jericho 941 also introduced a new caliber to the market, the .41 Action Express (or .41 AE), which was developed in 1986.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The .41 AE was a unique rebated rim cartridge designed to use .410 inch (10.25 mm) bullets and duplicate a reduced power police loading of the .41 Magnum.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The Jericho originally shipped with two barrels, one for 9×19mm Parabellum and the other for .41 AE.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Since the .41 AE was designed with a rebated rim the same dimensions as that of the 9 mm, the extractor and ejector worked equally well for either cartridge.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Ballistically, the .40 S&W was similar to moderate .41 AE loads (the reloading manuals that list the .41 AE generally say to use .40 S&W data), although commercial loadings of .41 AE were somewhat more powerful than the .40 S&W.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: With the stronger backing of major American firearms and ammunition manufacturers, the .40 S&W quickly pushed the .41 AE out of the market.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: The Jericho 941 was only on the market for one year before the dual 9 mm/.41 AE chambering was dropped, and the pistol was sold as either 9 mm or .40 S&W.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Some shooters like the Baby Eagle in .40 S&W for its extra reserve of strength, since the Baby Eagle was originally designed for a more potent cartridge (the .41 AE), which is the reverse of some makers who dropped .40 S&W barrels into pistols previously spec'd for the milder 9mm cartridge and had subsequent barrel and cartridge case failures.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: A later compact version, the Jericho 941, was chambered in .45 ACP or 9 mm.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: It is not clear to what extent the Jericho original 41.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: AE/9 mm Jericho frame and slide were "built-up" to handle the .45 ACP's much larger diameter; although the .45 ACP is a milder load running at much lower pressures than the .41 AE, the barrel opening for the .45 ACP is, of necessity, much larger.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Desert Eagle does not permit the use of +P or +P+ loads per its website FAQ, although reviewers have shot and reported the ballistics on +P .45 ACP defensive loadings.

title: Jericho 941R
sentence_text: Initially, Jericho pistols used barrels with polygonal rifling, which sometimes produces slightly higher velocity due to better bullet to barrel fit.

title: Jericho 941R

title: Jericho 941R

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: The Walther P88 is a semiautomatic pistol developed by the Walther company of Germany in 1988, hence the model name P88.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: Its main feature is a high-capacity double-stacked magazine designed for military and law enforcement use.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: It was seen in Beverly Hills Cop III.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: In 1996, Walther discontinued the pistol in favor of the slightly lighter and smaller P88 Compact, which was also cheaper to manufacture.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: The Walther P88 is highly prized among collectors and sport shooters due to its superb accuracy (1.5 to 2 inch groups at 25 yards) and high-quality construction.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: The Walther P88 Compact was lighter and a bit smaller with only minor differences.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: The standard P88 was heavily criticised for being bulky, heavy and expensive; the Compact solved none of these issues.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: There were many complaints about the full-size model's slide stop as well.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: It was thought to be hard to reach and manipulate, requiring the user to shift the gun in hand to operate.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: This was corrected with the P88 Compact at the expense of losing a very popular feature: its unique combination ambidextrous decocker and slide release.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: It was replaced with a CZ 99-style combination decocker and conventional slide stop/release.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: This allowed the pistol to function as a DAO (double action only) or conventional DA/SA.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: Both these pistols are fully ambidextrous with the exception of the compact's slide stop/release.

title: Walther P88
sentence_text: Full ambidextrous control was an industry first and the original P88 likely would have sold well were it not for the high cost.

title: Walther P88

Michael "Mikey" Tandino was a small-time crook and a close friend of Axel Foley from Beverly Hills Cop.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: Michael Tandino grew up with Axel Foley and Jenny Summers in the same Detroit neighborhood.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: In their teenage years, he and Foley would commit all sorts of mischief, including the criminal kind.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: At the age of fifteen, they stole a Cadillac, but were stopped by the police.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: Tandino was sentenced to youth custody, but out of loyalty to his friend he kept Foley safe from persecution.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: It was presumably this event that turned Axel's life around and got him started as a police detective.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: Mikey was arrested again for breaking into homes and sentenced to a longer prison stay.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: As an adult, Tandino evidently continued to live of small-time schemes until he was recommended by Summers to work as a security guard for the art dealer Victor Maitland in Beverly Hills.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: However, sometime before he went back to Detroit, he chanced upon a crate containing German bearer bonds lying unattended in one of Maitland's warehouses, and took some of the bonds with him.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: However, he was unaware that these bonds were part of a smuggling operation run by Maitland, who took extreme exception to the theft and sent his chief bodyguard Zack after Tandino.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: Tandino after meeting his end

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: Arriving in Detroit, Tandino broke into Foley's apartment to surprise his old friend.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: Upon their reunion, Tandino and Foley went out carousing, in which course Tandino told Foley about his new job and the bearer bonds he had swiped.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: When they arrived back at Foley's apartment, however, they found Zack and Casey waiting for them; Casey knocked Foley out, while Zack retrieved the stolen bonds and then executed Tandino by shooting him twice in the back of his head.

title: Mikey Tandino
sentence_text: Angered by the murder, Foley later went to Beverly Hills to investigate and avenge his best friend's death, setting the events of the film series in motion.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: Chief Harold Lutz was the overarching antagonist in Beverly Hills Cop II.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: He is the incompetent and verbally abusive chief of the Beverly Hills Police Department who frequently clashes with the protagonists throughout the movie.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: Harold Lutz is Chief of the Beverly Hills Police Department; the circumstances as to exactly how he attained this post along with his life beforehand is unknown.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: However, it soon became evident that Lutz chose a new "political" approach of appeasement - mainly to remain on the good side of Ted Egan, the mayor of Beverly Hills, who had hired him for this position, and prevent any "trouble-stirring" in Beverly Hills.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: For that purpose, in his two months of office, Lutz repeatedly clashed with Andrew Bogomil along with Billy Rosewood and John Taggart on frequent occasions and fired every other cop who served under Chief Hubbard, some of whom had served for decades and were close to a proper retirement.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: Lutz was also highly egotistical, refusing to take criticism and good advice and never acknowledging good police work if it left him out of the spotlight.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: He reacted angrily to when Rosewood contacted the local FBI office about the Alphabet Bandit, suspending Bogomil and putting Rosewood and Taggart on traffic duty as punishment, by using Mayor Egan's political career as an excuse.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: After Bogomil was shot by Karla Fry later on, Rosewood contacted their friend Axel Foley, who immediately rushed over to look after Bogomil and to take up the case.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: Because of the lingering threat of Lutz demoting or firing Rosewood and Taggert, Axel agreed to conduct the investigation undercover.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: In order to avoid any unnecessary interference from Lutz, he posed as a member of a fictional "multi-jurisdictional federal task force against organized crime", with some assistance from Jeffrey Friedman posing as Inspector Todd on the phone.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: After the robbery was foiled at the Bank depot (which unknown to Lutz was foiled by Foley, Rosewood and Taggert), Mayor Egan got a first glimpse of Lutz's abusive behavior firsthand during the meeting regarding the robbery, citing it was inexcusable and reminding him of the fact the robbery was prevented and the money was saved, before stating they should be focusing on learning where the next Alphabet Crime is going to be before leaving the police station.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: In anger after Mayor Egan left, Lutz had Foley thrown out since he wasn't really part of their department.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: After Charles Cain, the Alphabet Crimes' executive hand and secretly Maxwell Dent's pawn was later found dead at the scene of one of the Alphabet Heists and the code in the letters left at the crime scenes finally appeared to be cracked, Lutz promptly took credit for this "accomplishment" and announced the end of the Alphabet Crimes to the public.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: However, these letters were decoys to fool Lutz and the police; it was then Foley, Rosewood and Taggert finally uncovered the real motive behind those crimes - a major arms deal between Maxwell Dent and Nikos Thomopolis.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: After the gun fight and deaths of Dent and Fry, Lutz, arriving on the scene with Mayor Egan, was furious at Rosewood and Taggert for their insubordination and tried to have Foley arrested.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: However, this time Rosewood and Taggert finally stood up to Lutz, with Rosewood returning Lutz's continued abuse with an angry outburst and leaving Lutz baffled in front of Mayor Egan, who once again saw firsthand evidence of his incompetence by studying Thomopolis' notebook in which the deal was noted down.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: Instead of apologizing to Rosewood and Taggert, let alone commending them for their work, Lutz fired them just to be spiteful.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: But this proved to be the final straw for Mayor Egan, who promptly fired Lutz for jeopardizing the investigation and his abusive attitude.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: Lutz unsuccessfully attempted to talk his way out of it before Egan made it clear his decision was final, having now regretted making Lutz the Chief of Police in the first place.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: Lutz bitterly tells Egan that he'll regret it, only to be dismissed for his actions before Egan proceeds to fire Lutz's personal assistant Biddle moments later; Lutz then storms off in a temper tantrum and is followed by Biddle following the latter's dismissal.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: Shortly afterwards, Egan rehired Rosewood and Taggert before promoting the recovered Bogomil to replace Lutz as the new Chief of the Beverly Hills Police Department.

title: Harold Lutz
sentence_text: What happened to Lutz after he got fired is unknown, but it's likely he'll never get work for any police department due to his abusive attitude.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: Theodore "Ted" Egan was the mayor of Beverly Hills, California in Beverly Hills Cop II.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: Egan was a well-meaning wealthy politician and civic adminstrator who made his only mistake when he personally appointed Harold Lutz as the new chief of police for the Beverly Hills Police Department.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: Lutz did everything he could to stay on Egan's good side, including wrapping up unfinished cases and firing all the "disobedient" or potentially "trouble-shooting" cops who served under Chief Hubbard, some of whom had served for decades and were close to retirement.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: However, Lutz was in reality an egotistical and verbally abusive bully, who proved grossly incompetent when the city was hit by the Alphabet Crimes wave, which was the prelude for a major arms deal between Maxwell Dent and Nikos Thomopolis.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: After a robbery at the Bank depot was foiled in secret by Detroit detective Axel Foley and his Beverly Hills compatriots Billy Rosewood and John Taggart, Mayor Egan got a glimpse of Lutz's abusive behavior firsthand during the meeting regarding the robbery, citing it was inexcusable and reminding him of the fact that the robbery was prevented and the money was saved, and that they should be focusing on learning where the next Alphabet Crime was going to be.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: As Mayor Egan left, Lutz called him by his first name, to which Egan angrily responded "Mayor!", signifying he doesn't consider Lutz a friend.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: After Charles Cain, the Alphabet Crimes' executive hand and secretly Maxwell Dent's patsy was later found dead at the scene of one of the Alphabet Heists and the code in the letters left at the crime scenes finally appeared to be cracked, Lutz promptly took credit for this "accomplishment" and announced the end of the Alphabet Crimes to the public, to which Mayor Egan was present.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: However, these letters were decoys to fool Lutz, while Foley, Rosewood and Taggart came behind the truth and prevented the arms shipping.

title: Ted Egan
sentence_text: When Egan, Lutz and his assistant Biddle arrived, Rosewood and Taggart were instantly berated by Lutz for starting a firefight (disregarding the fact that this was done in self-defense).

title: Ted Egan

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The Mini-14, Mini Thirty, and Mini-6.8 are small, lightweight semi-automatic carbines manufactured by the U.S. firearms company Sturm, Ruger.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The Mini-14 non-target versions can fire both the .223 Remington cartridge and the similar military 5.56x45mm cartridge.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The target model Mini-14 rifles are chambered only for the .223 Remington cartridge.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The Mini Thirty uses the 7.62×39mm and the Mini-6.8 fires 6.8 mm Remington SPC.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The weapons are based off the .30-06 M-14 rifle but chambered for smaller calibers, hence the name "Mini-14".

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The Mini-14 was seen in Beverly Hills Cop II.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The Mini-14 was first introduced in 1974 by Ruger.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The name Mini-14 is derived from the military M14 rifle implying a miniature version of the M14.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: Ruger used the M14 as a model for the new rifle while incorporating numerous innovations and cost-saving engineering changes.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The Mini-14 proved popular with small-game hunters, ranchers, law enforcement, security personnel and target shooters.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: Designed by L.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: James Sullivan and William B.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: Ruger, the rifle employs an investment cast, heat-treated receiver and a version of the M1/M14 rifle locking mechanism with a self-cleaning, fixed-piston gas system.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The rifle is available in stainless or blued finish with hardwood, synthetic, or laminated stocks and an 18.5-inch (470 mm) barrel.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: Target models are currently available only in .223 Remington and are not chambered to fire the 5.56x45mm NATO round.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: They feature a 22-inch (560 mm) heavy barrel and either a laminated wood or Hogue overmolded synthetic stock.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: Most Mini-14s have a classic sporter appearance, in contrast to comparable autoloading rifles such as the AK-47 and AR-15.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: However Ruger now offers some Mini-14 rifles in a black ATI adjustable folding stock with a pistol grip.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: While the magazines of the Mini-14 resemble M16-style STANAG magazines, the two designs are not interchangeable.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: Initial rifles were produced with a complex, exposed bolt hold open device with no button for manual engagement.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: Stocks were somewhat angular and heat shields were made of wood.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: These rifles, with serial number prefixes before 181, were tooled and redesigned with a new stock, new bolt hold-open mechanism, and other small changes.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: In 2003, Ruger again overhauled the design and the production process to improve accuracy and update the styling while at the same time reducing production costs.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The new models, marketed as Ranch Rifles, are based on the previous Ranch models, with integral scope bases.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: In 2005, the new ranch rifles carried serial numbers beginning with 580.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: These rifles are sometimes referred to as 580 series ranch rifles.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: These new models use a modified gas system designed to reduce barrel vibration, and new iron sights.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: At an unspecified time in 2007 to 2008, Ruger added a heavier tapered barrel to the Mini series.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The heavier barrel had an overall larger diameter with the barrel visibly becoming thicker in the final inches as the barrel approaches the gas block from the muzzle.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: These changes combined with tighter tolerances result in greater potential accuracy.

title: Ruger Mini-14
sentence_text: The new mini-14 rifles are arguably capable of shooting under 2 MOA (Minute of angle) accuracy.

title: Ruger Mini-14

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: The Franchi SPAS-12 is a combat shotgun manufactured by Italian firearms company Franchi from 1979 to 2000.

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: One was owned by Billy Rosewood and used by John Taggart in Beverly Hills Cop II.

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: Only five percent (about 1,850) of the estimated SPAS-12 shotguns manufactured were imported into the United States.

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: The SPAS-12 is a dual-mode shotgun, adjustable for semi-automatic or pump-action operation.

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: The SPAS-12 was sold to military and police users worldwide, on the civilian market, and has been featured in many movies, TV shows and video games.

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: The appearance and intended purpose of the SPAS-12 initially led to its "Military" designation as a Combat Shotgun.

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: The SPAS-12 was designed from the ground up as a rugged military shotgun and it was named the (Special Purpose Automatic Shotgun).

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: In 1990 Franchi renamed the shotgun to the (Sporting Purpose Automatic Shotgun), this allowed continued sales to the U.S. as a limited-magazine-capacity, fixed-stock model until 1994.

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: Following the United States Federal Assault Weapons Ban, imports of SPAS-12 shotguns were stopped into the United States.

title: Franchi SPAS-12
sentence_text: In September 2004 the ban had expired, but Franchi had ended production in 2000 of the SPAS-12 to focus on the manufacturing of the SPAS-15 model.[citation needed] The SPAS-12's retail price in its final year of 2000 was $1500.00 USD averaged for final sales outside the U.S. to non-restricted countries.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: The Walther PP (Polizeipistole, or police pistol) series pistols are blowback-operated semi-automatic pistols, developed by the German arms manufacturer Walther.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: The Walther PPK was seen in Beverly Hills Cop III.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: The most common variant is the Walther PPK, the Polizeipistole Kriminalmodell (Police Pistol Detective Model), indicating it was more concealable than the original PP and hence better suited to plainclothes or undercover work.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: Kriminal refers to the police detective (criminal) division.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: Sometimes, the name Polizeipistole Kurz (Short Police Pistol) is used; however, the accuracy of that interpretation is unclear.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: The PPK is a smaller version of the PP (Polizeipistole) with a shorter grip and barrel and reduced magazine capacity.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: The PP and the PPK were among the world's first successful double action semi-automatic pistols that were widely copied, but still made by Walther.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: The design inspired other pistols, among them the Soviet Makarov, the Hungarian FEG PA-63, the Argentinian Bersa Thunder 380, the Swiss SIG P230, the German Mauser HSc, the Spanish Astra Constable, the American Jennings J-22 and Iver Johnson TP-22, and the Czech CZ50.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: Originally built in 1929, the Walther PPK remains a popular pistol used today for concealed carry, VIP protection, and various police, though usually superseded by newer designs.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: After more gun control laws, the PPK/S was created, which combined a PP slide with a PPK frame.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: It features an exposed hammer, a traditional double-action trigger mechanism, a single-column magazine, and a fixed barrel that also acts as the guide rod for the recoil spring.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: The series includes the Walther PP, PPK, PPK/S, and PPK/E.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: Various PP series are manufactured in either Germany, France or the United States.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: Since 2002, the PPK variant is solely manufactured by Smith & Wesson in Houlton, Maine, United States, under license from Carl Walther GmbH Sportwaffen.

title: Walther PPK
sentence_text: In the past, this particular model has been manufactured by Carl Walther in its own factory in Germany, as well as under licenses by Manurhin in Alsace, France, and by Interarms in Alexandria, Virginia, USA.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Orrin Sanderson is the secondary antagonist in Beverly Hills Cop III.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: He is a corrupt office manager of the famous theme park named Wonder World, where under the guise of printing corporate script, he takes part in the perfect cover up to manufacture counterfeit U.S. paper currency and smuggle them around the country with Ellis DeWald, the corrupt and murderous head of security who at the start of the film received a box truck full of stolen blank U.S.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Federal Reserve Notes by a criminal outfit operating a chop shop in [[Detroit]].

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: After being shown the stacks of notes, a jubilant DeWald ordered his men to shoot all the criminals in cold blood to leave no witnesses.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: A Detroit Police squad headed by Axel Foley (who knows nothing of the counterfeit money scheme or the fact that a massacre had taken place) arrives at the scene to peacefully arrest the car thieves without SWAT out of concern that they might compromise his evidence and lives being lost.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Things get violent when Foley confronts DeWald, who personally kills his Captain who arrived at the scene.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Axel followed him to Wonder World after his men had carelessly left behind evidence of Wonder World engraved towels.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: He sent his men to capture him, but Axel escaped.

sentence_text: After a frightening experience on the malfunctioning Spider ride, which Axel saved two children, DeWald's men caught him and brought him to the Sanderson.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Since Axel recognized him at once, but Jon Flint who had just arrived, refused to believe Axel's story since they were old friends.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Axel was lead away and Dewald told Flint to keep Axel way from the park.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Knowing Axel was now onto him, DeWald put all his men on alert to capture Axel if they should see him.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: However Axel disguised himself as one of the Wonder World mascots Okey Dokey and infiltrated the main room where the counterfeit operation was taking place.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: DeWald however, noticed Axel's reflection in one of the glass lights and sent his men to capture him, but Axel once again escaped.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: DeWald's men chased him and not wanting to put anyone in danger, Axel fired his gun in the air, placed it on the ground and surrendered.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: DeWald's men then took him away and Axel tried to convince Rosewood and Flint about the counterfeiting ring.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: DeWald and Sanderson willingly took them to the room, but had craftily hidden the counterfeit bills and replaced them with currency that would be used for the theme park, so once again Axel's story was unconvincing.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: DeWald then framed Axel for the shooting of Uncle Dave by broadcasting it on the news and then went back to the park where he took Janice hostage.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: However, Uncle Dave was able to inform on the news that DeWald is his true assassin.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: When Axel phoned, DeWald told him to meet him at the park, alone and unarmed.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: His terms were the mint paper for Janice's life.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Axel accepted and after he arrived, he was taken to the control room to see him.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: When Axel refuses to hand the mint paper over, DeWald threatens to kill them both, therefore making the paper useless.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Declaring to Sanderson who was holding Janice that he knows how cops think, threatens Axel into giving him the car keys as he knows that the note is hidden in the car.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Axel holds up a key chain and presses a button that gives out a blinding flash, stunning DeWald long enough to grab his gun and tell DeWald's chief guard to drop his weapon.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: Suddenly, Rosewood, who had been told before hand of Axel's intentions, appears and a massive fight and shootout takes place with the park attractions malfunctioning, Rosewood and Janice being trapped in a sealed room with a safety precaution cooling system and Axel escaping.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: He locks the crooks in by smashing the security lock.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: After a warning from Sanderson that Axel was using an outside line to get help, DeWald shoots the phone lines, leaving Axel to fend for himself.

title: Orrin Sanderson
sentence_text: He then sends out an alert to get Foley before his chief guard opens the door and they set off after the cop, while Sanderson is gunned down for some unknown reason by Fulbright.

sentence_text: Biddle is Chief Harold Lutz's assistant in Beverly Hills in Beverly Hills Cop II.

title: Biddle
sentence_text: Biddle was part of the Beverly Hills Police Department for two months as the assistant to the incompetent and verbally abusive new Police Chief Harold Lutz.

title: Biddle
sentence_text: When Mayor Ted Egan fired Lutz for his incompetence and abusive attitude, Biddle was fired, though he himself did nothing wrong.

title: Biddle
sentence_text: However, it is probable that he allowed Lutz's abusive behavior to continue by not taking appropriate measures to stop it.

sentence_text: Axel Foley claims that he "couldn't catch the Alphabet Killer if he nuked the city".

After Foley, Rosewood and Taggart manage to foil the City Deposit robbery, at the debriefing Biddle suddenly floats the theory that cement truck (that Rosewood and Foley followed the robbers in) was a tactical decoy, a diversion to protect the criminals' getaway, which was why it rammed the police car.

Nikos Thomopolis is an arms dealer and the quarternary antagonist in Beverly Hills Cop II.

title: Nikos Thomopolis
sentence_text: Thomopolis was an American citizen of Greek descent who worked himself up to become one of the biggest and wealthiest arms dealers on the American West Coast; sometimes obviously with subversive, illegal means.

title: Nikos Thomopolis
sentence_text: One day, Thomopolis entered a million dollar-heavy transaction for modern firearms with entrepeneur Maxwell Dent, whose businesses were on the verge of bankruptcy.

title: Nikos Thomopolis
sentence_text: Dent hoped to recuperate these losses by committing high-profile crimes to procure money, then use these funds to buy weapons from Thomopolis, and then resell those to his contacts in Central America at an inflated price.

title: Nikos Thomopolis
sentence_text: However, Captain Andrew Bogomil of the Beverly Hills Police Department got a hint and began investigating Dent's activities, forcing Dent and his lover Karla to try and eliminate him.

title: Nikos Thomopolis
sentence_text: This, on the other hand, called Detective Axel Foley of the Detroit Police Department, a friend of Bogomil, to the scene, and with the aid of Bogomil's subordinates and friends Taggart and Rosewood Foley began to unravel the case.

title: Nikos Thomopolis
sentence_text: By stealthily collecting hints and carefully analysing them, Foley eventually deduced the true motive behind Dent's activities, and together with Taggart and Rosewood intervened just as Dent and Thomopolis completed their deal.

title: Nikos Thomopolis
sentence_text: The three officers got into a firefight with Dent and Thomopolis' bodyguards until Foley and Rosewood destroyed the weapon shipments, whereupon Thomopolis and the rest of the henchmen surrendered and were arrested.

title: Nikos Thomopolis
sentence_text: Thomopolis' notebook, in which he had recorded the transaction, incidentally helped Beverly Hills mayor Ted Egan in cementing his decision to fire Harold Lutz, the incompetent new chief of the Beverly Hills Police, since his stubborn refusal to listen to the truth from his own men had jeopardized the investigation.

title: Charles Cain

title: Charles Cain

title: Sam Enriquez

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: Police Detective Billy Rosewood heard about the case and instantly believed there was more to the story, since he suspected Captain Cade Grant, the arresting officer, of being dirty.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: His Chief John Taggart and the detective assigned to the case Bobby Abbott didn't believe Rosewood, so Rosewood called his surrogate niece Jane Saunders to ask if she knew anyone in her law firm willing to take the case pro-bono - Jane volunteered herself.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: She went to meet Sam and explained she was there to help him.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: He eventually believed her and opened up about what occurred that night.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: She successfully petitioned the court for a change in counsel and made her motion to subpoena Copeland's financial records as she planned to claim he was a dirty cop.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: This angered many people, and she was attacked in her car as the assailants demanded she drop the case.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: The incident caused Rosewood to contact Jane's estranged father Axel Foley, who returned to Beverly Hills to help his daughter solve the case.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: Through days of investigative work, they learned Captain Grant had enlisted Copeland to help run drugs through Beverly Hills.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: Copeland wanted out of the business and was going to blow the cover on the operation, so Grant had him killed.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: He then staged the car to make it appear Sam was the murderer.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: However, he didn't expect Copeland to have a low-grade security camera in the car that captured the attack.

title: Sam Enriquez
sentence_text: When Axel, Jane, and Bobby found the SD card containing the video, they successfully freed Sam.

title: Chalino Valdemoro
sentence_text: A drug cartel leader in Beverly Hills, Chalino's operation is massive and impenetrable.

title: Chalino Valdemoro
sentence_text: Chalino pressures his nephew Sam Enriquez into muling drugs for him with another member named Copeland, unaware that Copeland is an undercover cop.
